column3: Event Description
After Moses’ death, the LORD directly commissions Joshua to lead Israel across the Jordan.
Joshua commands the officers to prepare the people to cross the Jordan within three days.
Joshua reminds the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh of their obligation to help conquer the land.
Joshua sends two spies from Shittim to view the land, especially Jericho.
The spies lodge in the house of Rahab the prostitute, where she hides them from the king of Jericho.
Rahab confesses faith in the LORD and makes a covenant with the spies for the safety of her household.
Rahab lets the spies down by a rope through the window, and they instruct her to hang a scarlet cord.
The spies hide in the hills for three days and then return to Joshua with a favorable report.
Israel departs from Shittim and camps on the banks of the Jordan before crossing.
Joshua instructs the people to follow the ark at a distance and to consecrate themselves.
The LORD promises to exalt Joshua that day as the priests bearing the ark step into the Jordan.
The waters of the Jordan miraculously stop flowing when the priests’ feet touch the water.
The LORD commands Joshua to take twelve men, one from each tribe, to remove stones from the riverbed.
Twelve stones are set up as a memorial at Gilgal, and twelve stones are placed in the middle of the Jordan.
The priests carrying the ark come up from the Jordan, and the waters return to flood stage.
The memorial at Gilgal is explained to future generations as a sign of God’s power.
The kings of the Amorites and Canaanites lose heart because of the miracle at the Jordan.
The LORD commands Joshua to circumcise the new generation born in the wilderness.
Israel celebrates the Passover on the fourteenth day of the month at Gilgal.
The manna ceases the day after the Passover when Israel eats the produce of Canaan.
Joshua encounters the Commander of the army of the LORD near Jericho and worships him.
The LORD gives Joshua detailed instructions for marching around Jericho for seven days.
Israel marches around Jericho once each day for six days with priests blowing rams’ horns.
On the seventh day Israel marches seven times, shouts, and the walls of Jericho collapse.
Joshua spares Rahab and her household and burns Jericho, devoting everything to destruction.
Joshua pronounces a curse on anyone who rebuilds Jericho.
Achan secretly takes devoted items from Jericho, causing the LORD’s anger against Israel.
Joshua sends men to spy out Ai, but Israel is routed and thirty-six men are killed.
Joshua and the elders mourn and intercede before the ark because of the defeat.
The LORD reveals that Israel has sinned by taking devoted things and commands their removal.
By lot, Achan is identified as the sinner and confesses to taking a mantle, silver, and gold.
Achan and his family and possessions are stoned and burned in the Valley of Achor.
The LORD encourages Joshua and gives a new strategy using an ambush to take Ai.
Joshua selects thirty thousand men and positions five thousand as an ambush west of Ai.
Joshua and the main force pretend to flee, drawing the king of Ai in pursuit.
The ambush force rises, captures Ai, and sets it on fire.
The Israelites turn and destroy the army of Ai, killing the king and all twelve thousand inhabitants.
Joshua builds an altar of uncut stones on Mount Ebal as Moses commanded.
Joshua offers burnt offerings and peace offerings and writes a copy of the Law on the stones.
Joshua reads the entire Law of Moses aloud to all Israel on Mounts Gerizim and Ebal.
The Gibeonites disguise themselves as travelers from a distant land to deceive Israel.
Joshua and the leaders make a covenant of peace with the Gibeonites without seeking the LORD’s counsel.
Three days later Israel discovers Gibeon is nearby, but the oath binds them.
The Gibeonites are made woodcutters and water carriers for the congregation and altar.
Five Amorite kings form a coalition and attack Gibeon because of its treaty with Israel.
Gibeon sends an urgent appeal to Joshua for help against the five kings.
Joshua marches all night from Gilgal, and the LORD promises victory.
The LORD throws the enemy into panic, and Israel slaughters them with great hailstones.
Joshua prays, and the sun stands still over Gibeon and the moon over Aijalon until victory is complete.
The five kings hide in a cave at Makkedah and are sealed inside.
Joshua has the kings brought out, places the feet of the leaders on their necks, and executes them.
Joshua captures and destroys Makkedah, Libnah, Lachish, Eglon, Hebron, and Debir in rapid succession.
Joshua defeats the entire southern region and returns to Gilgal.
Jabin king of Hazor forms a vast northern coalition with many horses and chariots.
The LORD promises to give the northern kings into Israel’s hand and commands hamstringing their horses.
Israel defeats the coalition at the waters of Merom and burns only Hazor.
Summary statement of the complete conquest of the land as Moses and the LORD commanded.
Comprehensive list of the thirty-one kings defeated by Israel west and east of the Jordan.
The LORD tells the aged Joshua that much land remains to be possessed.
Land east of the Jordan is allocated to Reuben, Gad, and half-tribe of Manasseh as Moses gave it.
Caleb recounts Moses’ promise and requests Hebron at age eighty-five.
Joshua blesses Caleb and gives him Hebron as his inheritance.
The tribe of Judah receives its inheritance by lot, with detailed boundaries.
Caleb drives out the three sons of Anak and gives his daughter Achsah to Othniel.
The cities of Judah are listed, including those in the Negeb and the failure to drive out Jerusalem.
The tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh (Joseph) receive their inheritance with boundaries.
The Joseph tribes complain that their portion is too small; Joshua tells them to clear the hill country.
The tabernacle is set up at Shiloh, and the remaining seven tribes are rebuked for delay.
Seven portions are surveyed and brought back to Shiloh for casting lots.
Benjamin receives its inheritance by lot.
Simeon receives its inheritance within the territory of Judah.
Zebulun, Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and Dan receive their inheritances by lot.
Joshua is given the city of Timnath-serah in the hill country of Ephraim as his personal inheritance.
Six cities of refuge are appointed: Kedesh, Shechem, Hebron, Bezer, Ramoth-Gilead, and Golan.
Forty-eight cities with their pasturelands are assigned to the Levites by lot.
The LORD has given Israel rest and fulfilled every promise made to the fathers.
Joshua blesses and dismisses the eastern tribes (Reuben, Gad, half-Manasseh) to their land.
The eastern tribes build a large altar by the Jordan, causing alarm among the western tribes.
Phinehas and ten leaders confront the eastern tribes, fearing rebellion against the LORD.
The eastern tribes explain that the altar is a witness, not for sacrifice, to preserve unity.
Phinehas and the leaders accept the explanation, and civil war is averted.
In his old age, Joshua gathers Israel and exhorts them to fear the LORD and put away foreign gods.
Joshua assembles all Israel at Shechem, reviews God’s faithfulness from Abraham to the conquest.
Joshua challenges Israel to choose whom they will serve and declares “As for me and my house…”
The people vow to serve the LORD, and Joshua warns them that God will not forgive apostasy.
Joshua makes a covenant with the people at Shechem, writes it in the Book of the Law, and sets a stone.
Joshua dies at age 110 and is buried in Timnath-serah.
The bones of Joseph, brought from Egypt, are buried at Shechem.
Eleazar son of Aaron dies and is buried at Gibeah.
After Joshua’s death, Israel asks the LORD which tribe should first attack the Canaanites.
Judah is chosen and joins with Simeon to fight the Canaanites and Perizzites.
Judah captures Bezek, cuts off the thumbs and big toes of Adoni-bezek, and he dies in Jerusalem.
Judah captures Jerusalem and sets it on fire.
Judah defeats the Canaanites in the hill country, Negeb, and lowland, including Hebron and Debir.
Caleb gives his daughter Achsah to Othniel, who receives the upper and lower springs.
The Kenites settle among the people of Judah in the Negeb.
Judah fails to drive out the inhabitants of the plain because they had iron chariots.
The house of Joseph (Ephraim and Manasseh) captures Bethel with the LORD’s help.
Israel fails to completely drive out the Canaanites in multiple regions, leaving them as forced laborers.
The angel of the LORD rebukes Israel at Bokim for making covenants with the inhabitants and not tearing down altars.
The generation that knew the LORD dies, and a new generation worships Baal and Ashtaroth.
The LORD’s anger burns against Israel; He gives them into the hand of plunderers and sells them to enemies.
The LORD raises up judges who save Israel, but the people return to corruption after each judge dies.
The LORD leaves certain nations to test Israel and to teach war to the new generation.
Israel intermarries with the remaining nations and serves their gods; the LORD gives them to Cushan-rishathaim.
Othniel son of Kenaz defeats Cushan-rishathaim king of Mesopotamia, and the land has rest forty years.
Eglon king of Moab oppresses Israel for eighteen years with the help of Ammon and Amalek.
Ehud the left-handed Benjamite is sent with tribute to Eglon and assassinates him in his roof chamber.
Ehud escapes, rallies Israel, and slaughters ten thousand Moabites; the land has rest eighty years.
Shamgar son of Anath kills six hundred Philistines with an oxgoad and saves Israel.
Israel again does evil, and the LORD sells them to Jabin king of Canaan, whose commander is Sisera.
Deborah the prophetess judges Israel at the Palm of Deborah and summons Barak to fight Sisera.
Heber the Kenite separates from his people and pitches his tent near Kedesh, informing Sisera of Barak’s movement.
Barak and ten thousand men go to Mount Tabor; Deborah declares that the LORD has gone out before him.
Sisera flees on foot to the tent of Jael the wife of Heber the Kenite.
Jael kills Sisera with a tent peg while he sleeps and shows his body to Barak.
God subdues Jabin king of Canaan, and Israel destroys him completely.
Deborah and Barak sing a lengthy song celebrating the victory and Jael’s deed.
Israel again does evil after Ehud’s death; Midian oppresses them for seven years.
The prophet rebukes Israel for abandoning the LORD who brought them out of Egypt.
The angel of the LORD appears to Gideon while he is threshing wheat in a winepress.
Gideon questions why calamity has come if the LORD is with them and asks for a sign with the offering.
The LORD commands Gideon to tear down his father’s altar to Baal and Asherah pole at night.
The men of Ophrah demand Gideon’s death, but Joash defends him, renaming him Jerubbaal.
Midian, Amalek, and eastern peoples invade; Gideon blows a trumpet and summons tribes.
Gideon asks for the sign of the wet fleece on dry ground, and it happens.
Gideon asks for the reverse sign (dry fleece on wet ground), and the LORD grants it.
The LORD reduces Gideon’s army from 32,000 to 300 by the manner in which they drink water.
Gideon overhears a Midianite’s dream and its interpretation, confirming victory, and worships God.
Gideon divides the 300 men into three companies, gives them trumpets and empty jars with torches, and surrounds the camp.
At Gideon’s signal the 300 blow trumpets, break jars, and shout; the Midianites panic and flee.
Men of Naphtali, Asher, and Manasseh pursue Midian; Ephraim captures the waters and kills Oreb and Zeeb.
Ephraim is angry for not being called earlier; Gideon appeases them by praising their victory.
Gideon and his 300 pursue Zebah and Zalmunna across the Jordan; Succoth and Penuel refuse bread and are punished later.
Gideon defeats the Midianite camp, captures kings Zebah and Zalmunna, and returns to punish Succoth and Penuel.
Gideon refuses to rule over Israel but asks for gold earrings from the spoil to make an ephod.
The land has rest forty years in Gideon’s days, but after his death Israel again worships Baal-berith.
Abimelech, Gideon’s son by a concubine, persuades Shechem to make him king and kills his seventy brothers.
Jotham escapes, stands on Mount Gerizim, and pronounces a parable and curse against Abimelech and Shechem.
God sends an evil spirit between Abimelech and Shechem; Gaal leads a revolt.
Zebul warns Abimelech, who defeats Gaal and destroys Shechem, sowing it with salt.
Abimelech attacks Thebez; a woman drops a millstone on his head, and he dies by his armor-bearer’s sword.
Tola son of Puah of Issachar judges Israel twenty-three years.
Jair the Gileadite judges Israel twenty-two years and has thirty sons who ride thirty donkeys.
Israel again worships foreign gods; the LORD sells them to Philistines and Ammonites for eighteen years.
Israel cries to the LORD, who initially refuses help but finally relents because of their misery.
The Ammonites encamp in Gilead; the elders of Gilead seek a leader against them.
Jephthah the Gileadite, a mighty warrior and son of a prostitute, is driven out but later recalled as leader.
Jephthah sends messengers to the king of Ammon, recounting Israel’s history and claiming the land by right.
The Spirit of the LORD comes upon Jephthah; he vows that whatever comes out of his house will be the LORD’s.
Jephthah defeats Ammon with a great slaughter; his daughter, his only child, greets him with timbrels.
Jephthah fulfills his vow concerning his daughter, who remains a virgin; she bewails her virginity two months.
Ephraim threatens Jephthah for not calling them; he gathers Gilead and defeats Ephraim at the Jordan fords.
Jephthah judges Israel six years and is buried in Gilead.
Ibzan of Bethlehem judges Israel seven years and has thirty sons and thirty daughters.
Elon the Zebulunite judges Israel ten years.
Abdon son of Hillel the Pirathonite judges Israel eight years and has forty sons and thirty grandsons.
Israel again does evil; the LORD gives them to the Philistines for forty years.
The angel of the LORD appears to Manoah’s barren wife and announces she will bear a son who is to be a Nazirite.
Manoah prays; the angel returns, repeats the instructions, and refuses to eat or reveal his name.
Samson is born; his mother names him Samson, and the Spirit of the LORD begins to stir him.
Samson demands a Philistine woman from Timnah as his wife despite his parents’ objection.
A young lion attacks Samson; the Spirit enables him to tear it apart, and later he finds honey in its carcass.
At the wedding feast Samson poses a riddle; the Philistines threaten his wife to get the answer.
Samson kills thirty men of Ashkelon for their garments to pay the wager, then returns angry to his father’s house.
Samson visits his wife with a young goat but is denied; her father gives her to the best man.
Samson catches three hundred foxes, ties torches to their tails, and burns the Philistine grain and vineyards.
The Philistines burn Samson’s wife and father-in-law; Samson slaughters many Philistines in revenge.
Three thousand men of Judah bind Samson and deliver him to the Philistines at Lehi.
The Spirit comes upon Samson; he breaks the ropes and kills a thousand Philistines with a donkey’s jawbone.
Samson calls on the LORD for water; God splits the hollow at Lehi, and the place is called En-hakkore.
Samson judges Israel twenty years in the days of the Philistines.
Samson goes to Gaza, visits a prostitute, and carries off the city gates to Hebron at midnight.
Samson falls in love with Delilah in the Valley of Sorek; the Philistine lords bribe her to discover his strength.
Delilah repeatedly asks the secret of Samson’s strength; he gives three false answers while Philistines try to seize him.
Delilah nags Samson until he reveals that his uncut hair is the secret of his Nazirite vow.
Delilah cuts Samson’s hair while he sleeps; the Philistines seize him, gouge out his eyes, and imprison him.
Samson’s hair begins to grow again; at a great feast to Dagon the Philistines bring him out to entertain them.
Samson prays for strength one last time, pushes the pillars of the temple of Dagon, and kills thousands in his death.
Samson’s brothers bury him between Zorah and Eshtaol; he had judged Israel twenty years.
Micah of Ephraim makes a shrine, an ephod, household gods, and installs his son as priest.
A wandering Levite from Bethlehem becomes Micah’s personal priest for ten shekels a year and clothing.
Five Danite spies pass Micah’s house, inquire of the Levite, and receive a favorable oracle for Laish.
The five spies discover Laish is prosperous and defenseless; they return and report to the tribe.
Six hundred armed Danites pass Micah’s house; they steal the ephod, idols, and Levite priest.
Micah pursues the Danites but turns back when threatened.
The Danites attack and burn Laish, rename it Dan, and set up Micah’s carved image and priesthood.
A Levite from Ephraim takes a concubine from Bethlehem who later leaves him and returns to her father’s house.
The Levite retrieves his concubine; they depart late and lodge in Gibeah of Benjamin.
Wicked men of Gibeah surround the house and demand the Levite; the host offers his daughter and the concubine.
The men abuse the concubine all night; she dies at the door at dawn.
The Levite cuts the concubine’s body into twelve pieces and sends them throughout Israel.
All Israel gathers at Mizpah, horrified, and vows not to return home until the crime is avenged.
The Benjamites refuse to surrender the guilty men of Gibeah and muster 26,700 swordsmen.
Israel inquires of God; Judah attacks first and loses 22,000 men on the first day.
Israel weeps, inquires again, attacks the second day, and loses 18,000 men.
Israel weeps at Bethel, fasts, offers sacrifices, and Phinehas inquires; God promises victory on the third day.
Israel uses an ambush strategy, draws Benjamin out, and destroys almost the entire tribe (25,100 men).
Israel mourns that one tribe is nearly cut off and swears at Mizpah never to give daughters to Benjamin.
Israel discovers Jabesh-gilead did not join the assembly and slaughters all but 400 virgins, giving them to Benjamin.
To provide wives for the remaining 200 Benjamites, Israel instructs them to seize girls dancing at Shiloh’s feast.
In those days there was no king in Israel; everyone did what was right in his own eyes.
In the days when the judges ruled, there was a famine in the land of Judah.
Elimelech, a man from Bethlehem in Judah, takes his wife Naomi and two sons Mahlon and Chilion to live in Moab.
Elimelech dies in Moab, leaving Naomi with her two sons.
Mahlon and Chilion marry Moabite women, Orpah and Ruth, and live in Moab about ten years.
Both Mahlon and Chilion die, leaving Naomi without husband or sons.
Naomi hears that the LORD has visited His people and given them food, so she prepares to return to Bethlehem.
Naomi tells her daughters-in-law to return to their mothers’ houses and prays the LORD will deal kindly with them.
Orpah kisses Naomi and returns to her people, but Ruth clings to Naomi.
Ruth makes her famous vow: “Where you go I will go… your people shall be my people, and your God my God.”
Naomi and Ruth arrive in Bethlehem at the beginning of the barley harvest.
Ruth the Moabite asks Naomi’s permission to glean in the fields behind anyone in whose eyes she finds favor.
Ruth “happens” to come to the portion of the field belonging to Boaz, a relative of Elimelech.
Boaz arrives from Bethlehem, greets his workers with “The LORD be with you,” and they reply similarly.
Boaz notices Ruth and asks the foreman who she is.
Boaz invites Ruth to glean only in his field, stay close to his servant girls, and drink from the water jars.
Ruth bows and asks why she has found favor in Boaz explains he has heard of her kindness to Naomi.
At mealtime Boaz invites Ruth to eat with the harvesters and gives her roasted grain.
Boaz instructs his young men to let Ruth glean even among the sheaves and to pull out some stalks for her.
Ruth gleans until evening, beats out what she has gathered—an ephah of barley—and brings it to Naomi.
Naomi identifies Boaz as one of their kinsman-redeemers.
Ruth continues gleaning in Boaz’s field until the end of the barley and wheat harvests.
Naomi instructs Ruth to wash, anoint herself, put on her cloak, and go to the threshing floor at night.
Ruth goes to the threshing floor, uncovers Boaz’s feet after he lies down, and lies at his feet.
At midnight Boaz awakens, discovers Ruth at his feet, and she asks him to spread his wing over her.
Boaz blesses Ruth for her kindness and promises to do the part of kinsman-redeemer if the nearer one will not.
Boaz gives Ruth six measures of barley so she will not return empty-handed to Naomi.
Ruth returns before dawn, tells Naomi everything, and Naomi says to wait and see what happens.
Boaz goes to the city gate and waits until the nearer kinsman-redeemer passes by.
Boaz calls ten elders of the city to sit as witnesses.
Boaz tells the nearer redeemer about Naomi’s land and the duty to redeem it.
The nearer redeemer agrees to redeem the land until he learns he must also marry Ruth the Moabite.
The nearer redeemer removes his sandal and formally relinquishes his right of redemption to Boaz.
Boaz announces before the elders and people that he is acquiring Ruth as wife to perpetuate name of the dead.
The elders and all at the gate bless Boaz and pray that Ruth may be like Rachel, Leah, and Tamar.
Boaz takes Ruth as his wife, the LORD enables her to conceive, and she bears a son.
The women of Bethlehem bless Naomi and declare the child a restorer of life and nourisher of her old age.
The women name the child Obed and declare he is son to Naomi.
The book concludes with the genealogy from Perez to David (Perez → Hezron → Ram → Amminadab → Nahshon → Salmon → Boaz → Obed → Jesse → David)
